literal: that which holds, sustains, supports; from √dhṛ 'to hold, to bear'
invariant_definition: There is no single shared invariant — *dharma* is the most polysemic of all Vedāntic terms. The unifying root sense is 'that which holds together, that which sustains.' This produces multiple specialized uses: (1) the right ordering of action conformable to *śruti* and *smṛti* — the Mīmāṃsā / *dharmaśāstra* sense of enjoined duty; (2) the lawful order of the cosmos (*ṛta*-successor); (3) the constituent quality of a thing in technical Nyāya–Vaiśeṣika usage (a *dharma* of a *dharmin* = a property of its substance-bearer); (4) in Buddhist usage (relevant by polemical contact), the elementary phenomenal constituent. In Vedānta the principal contested sense is (1) and its relation to *mokṣa*: whether *dharma* is preparatory, continuous, or transcended in the realized state.

per_school.definition: *Dharma* in the *karma-kāṇḍa* sense (enjoined ritual and ethical action) is preparatory: it produces *citta-śuddhi* but cannot of itself produce *mokṣa*. The realized one is *atikrānta-vidhi-niṣedha* — beyond enjoinment and prohibition (Bṛhad-Bhāṣya 4.4) — though continuing externally to act in conformity with *dharma*. *Dharma* in the metaphysical-property sense (4 above) is what *brahman*, properly described, lacks (*nirdharmaka brahman*).

*Dharma* in the enjoined-action sense remains binding even on the *prapanna* and on the liberated, because it is *bhagavad-ārādhana-rūpa* — itself a form of worship of the Lord. The *varṇa-āśrama-dharma* is permanently authoritative; the path of total surrender (*prapatti*) does not abolish but transfigures it.

*Dharma* is the eternal lawful structure of the cosmos established and maintained by Viṣṇu. *Varṇa-āśrama-dharma* is metaphysically real and intrinsic to the *jīva*'s nature (*svabhāva*); transgression incurs real *karma* and real consequences in the *taratamya*-graded cosmos.

Above all *varṇa-āśrama-dharma* stands *bhāgavata-dharma* — the dharma of devotion to Kṛṣṇa, accessible to all regardless of *varṇa* or *jāti*. Caitanya's revolutionary move is the public assertion that *bhāgavata-dharma* is everyone's *dharma* and that *varṇa-āśrama* is preparatory and dispensable for those whose *bhakti* has matured.

translator_note: Translating *dharma* as 'religion' is the standard colonial-era mistake (encoded in the British category 'the Hindu religion'). It is also wrongly translated as 'duty', 'law', 'righteousness', 'morality', or 'virtue' — each captures one slice. The correct policy is to leave *dharma* untranslated and to specify the sense from context. The user's position-brief is explicit: *dharma* is the lawful normative structure of cosmos and conduct, not 'religion' in the modern Western sense (which has no good Sanskrit equivalent and was constructed by 19th-century European Indologists).
